lessons.title: Rules of Sudoku
lessons.description: Learn the basic rules of Sudoku — what the grid is, what the goal is, and what's allowed.

steps.title: The Grid
steps.description: Sudoku is played on a 9×9 grid, divided into nine 3×3 boxes. Your goal is to fill every empty cell with a number from 1 to 9.

steps.title: The Three Rules
steps.description:
There are three rules:
1. Each row must contain 1-9 with no repeats
2. Each column must contain 1-9 with no repeats
3. Each 3×3 box must contain 1-9 with no repeats
That's it! The puzzle starts with some numbers already filled in (called 'givens'). You fill in the rest.

steps.title: No Repeating Numbers
steps.description: If you place a 5 in a row, you cannot place another 5 in that same row. Same for columns and boxes. This is the core constraint that makes Sudoku a logic puzzle.

steps.title: Try It: Place a Number
steps.description: Look at the highlighted cell. What number can go there? Look at the row, column, and box to figure out what's missing. Click the cell and enter the number.

steps.title: Givens vs User Numbers
steps.description: Numbers that come pre-filled are called 'givens' — they cannot be changed. Numbers you enter are your guesses. In this app, givens are bold and your numbers are shown in a different color.

lessons.title: Scanning Technique
lessons.description: Learn the most fundamental solving technique — scanning rows and columns to find where numbers can go.

steps.title: What is Scanning?
steps.description: Scanning is the process of looking at a row, column, or box and checking which numbers are already present. If 8 of 9 numbers are present, the missing one is obvious. Scanning is how you'll find most of your early solves.

steps.title: Scanning a Row
steps.description: Look at the highlighted row. It has: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9. What's missing? Place it in the empty cell.

steps.title: Scanning a Box
steps.description: Now look at the highlighted 3×3 box. Check which numbers are already in it, and which number from 1-9 is missing. Place it in the highlighted cell.

lessons.title: Naked Singles
lessons.description: When only one number can go in a cell, it's called a 'naked single'. Learn to spot them.

steps.title: What is a Naked Single?
steps.description: A naked single is a cell where only one number is possible. You find it by checking the row, column, and box for that cell — if 8 different numbers are already present across all three, only one number remains.

steps.title: Find the Naked Single
steps.description: Look at the highlighted cell. Check its row, column, and 3×3 box. What's the only number that can go there? Place it!

lessons.title: Hidden Singles
lessons.description: A hidden single is a number that can only go in one cell within a row, column, or box — even if other numbers could go in that cell too.

steps.title: What is a Hidden Single?
steps.description: Unlike a naked single (where the cell has only one candidate), a hidden single is where a NUMBER has only one possible cell. You scan a row/column/box and ask: 'Where can the number 7 go?' If only one cell in that unit can hold 7, that's where it goes.

steps.title: Find the Hidden Single
steps.description: Look at the top-left 3×3 box. Where can the number 7 go? Check each empty cell — does the row or column already contain a 7? Find the only cell where 7 fits.

lessons.title: Naked Pairs
lessons.description: When two cells in a row, column, or box can only be the same two numbers, those numbers are locked — they can't appear elsewhere in that unit.

steps.title: What is a Naked Pair?
steps.description: A naked pair occurs when two cells in the same row, column, or box have exactly the same two candidates — and no others. For example, if two cells can only be {2, 7}, then 2 and 7 are locked to those cells. You can remove 2 and 7 from all other cells in that row/column/box.

steps.title: Spot the Naked Pair
steps.description: Look at the highlighted row. Two cells have pencil marks showing only {3, 5}. Since these two cells must contain 3 and 5 (in some order), no other cell in that row can be 3 or 5. This narrows down the candidates for the remaining cells!

steps.title: Using Naked Pairs to Eliminate
steps.description: Once you identify a naked pair, you can eliminate those candidates from other cells. This often reveals naked singles or other patterns. The key insight: the pair 'reserves' those two numbers for those two cells — no one else in that unit can use them.

lessons.title: Hidden Pairs
lessons.description: When two numbers can only go in the same two cells within a unit, those cells are locked to those numbers — even if other candidates exist.

steps.title: What is a Hidden Pair?
steps.description: A hidden pair is the complement of a naked pair. Instead of looking at what candidates a cell HAS, you look at where a NUMBER can go. If two numbers (say, 3 and 7) can each only go in the same two cells in a row, then those two cells must contain 3 and 7. All other candidates in those cells can be eliminated.

steps.title: Find the Hidden Pair
steps.description: Look at the top row. Where can the number 4 go? Where can the number 9 go? If they can only go in the same two cells, those cells form a hidden pair. You can then eliminate all other candidates from those cells. Click the highlighted cell and enter the number 4.

steps.title: Why Hidden Pairs Matter
steps.description: Hidden pairs are powerful because they let you eliminate candidates you might not have known were wrong. By removing extra candidates, you often reveal naked singles or simpler patterns. The key is to look at WHERE numbers can go, not just WHAT a cell can contain.

lessons.title: Pointing Pairs
lessons.description: When a number's candidates in a box are all in the same row or column, that number can be eliminated from the rest of that row/column.

steps.title: What is a Pointing Pair?
steps.description: Sometimes a number can only go in cells that are all in the same row or column within a 3×3 box. When this happens, that number MUST be in that row/column within the box — so it can't be anywhere else in that row/column outside the box. You can eliminate those candidates.

steps.title: Spot the Pointing Pair
steps.description: Look at the top-left 3×3 box. If the number 5 can only go in cells in the first row of the box (cells (0,0), (0,1), or (0,2)), then 5 must be in row 0 within this box. You can eliminate 5 from all other cells in row 0.

steps.title: Pointing Pairs Eliminate Candidates
steps.description: After eliminating the pointed-away candidates, you often reveal new singles or pairs. Pointing pairs are one of the most commonly used intermediate techniques because they appear frequently in real puzzles.

lessons.title: X-Wing Technique
lessons.description: When a number can only go in two positions in two different rows (or columns), and those positions align, you can eliminate that number from other cells in the intersecting columns (or rows).

steps.title: What is an X-Wing?
steps.description:
An X-Wing is a pattern where a specific number has exactly two candidate positions in each of two rows, and those positions are in the same two columns. This forms a rectangle (the 'X-Wing'). Since the number must appear in both rows, and it can only go in those two columns, the number is locked to those four cells. You can eliminate it from all other cells in those two columns.

The name comes from the X-shaped pattern formed by the two rows and two columns intersecting.

steps.title: The X-Wing Rule
steps.description:
If number N can only go in columns C1 and C2 in both row R1 and row R2 (exactly two candidates per row), then N must be at (R1,C1) or (R1,C2), AND at (R2,C1) or (R2,C2). This means N is locked to these four cells. Eliminate N from all other cells in columns C1 and C2.

The same logic works with rows and columns swapped.

steps.title: Why X-Wing is Powerful
steps.description:
X-Wing is a 'fish' pattern — the simplest of the fish family (which includes Swordfish and Jellyfish). It eliminates candidates across entire rows/columns, often breaking through tough puzzles that scanning and pairs can't solve.

Key tip: Always look for X-Wings when a number has very few candidates remaining in a row or column.

lessons.title: Swordfish Technique
lessons.description: An extension of X-Wing using three rows and three columns instead of two.

steps.title: What is a Swordfish?
steps.description:
A Swordfish is like an X-Wing but with three rows and three columns instead of two. If a number has at most three candidate positions in each of three rows, and all those positions fall within the same three columns, then you can eliminate that number from all other cells in those three columns.

The pattern looks like a 3×3 grid of candidate cells (though not all 9 need to be filled — a minimum of 3 per row is needed).

steps.title: When to Look for Swordfish
steps.description:
Swordfish patterns are rare but extremely powerful. Look for them when:
- A number has very few candidates remaining
- You see X-Wing-like alignment across three rows/columns
- The puzzle is hard (difficulty 50+)

Most puzzles won't need Swordfish, but when they do, it's often the only way forward without guessing.

lessons.title: XY-Wing Technique
lessons.description: A three-cell pattern that eliminates candidates based on a chain of bivalue cells.

steps.title: What is an XY-Wing?
steps.description:
An XY-Wing involves three cells: a pivot cell with candidates {X, Y}, and two wing cells — one with {X, Z} and one with {Y, Z}. The pivot sees both wings. Regardless of whether the pivot is X or Y, one of the wings must be Z. Therefore, any cell that sees BOTH wings can have Z eliminated.

The 'sees' relationship means sharing a row, column, or box.

steps.title: Finding the Pivot
steps.description:
To find an XY-Wing:
1. Find a bivalue cell (only 2 candidates) — this is your pivot, with candidates {X, Y}
2. Find another bivalue cell that shares a unit with the pivot and has {X, Z} — this is wing 1
3. Find a third bivalue cell that shares a unit with the pivot and has {Y, Z} — this is wing 2
4. Any cell that sees BOTH wing 1 and wing 2 can have Z eliminated

The beauty: you don't need to know if the pivot is X or Y — the elimination works either way!

steps.title: XY-Wing in Practice
steps.description:
XY-Wing is one of the most satisfying techniques to find. It often appears in puzzles rated 'Hard' and 'Expert'. The key is to scan for bivalue cells (cells with exactly 2 pencil marks) and check if they form the pivot-wing pattern.

Tip: Use the auto-notes feature to fill pencil marks, then look for cells with only 2 candidates to find potential pivots and wings.

lessons.title: Unique Rectangle
lessons.description: Exploit the fact that valid Sudoku puzzles have exactly one solution to eliminate impossible candidates.

steps.title: The Uniqueness Principle
steps.description:
Every valid Sudoku puzzle has exactly ONE solution. This means certain patterns can NEVER appear in a valid puzzle. If a pattern would create multiple solutions, at least one of its candidates must be wrong — and you can eliminate it.

The most common uniqueness technique is the Unique Rectangle (UR).

steps.title: What is a Unique Rectangle?
steps.description:
A Unique Rectangle occurs when four cells form a rectangle (same two rows, same two columns) and three of them have the same two candidates {A, B}. If the fourth cell also had only {A, B}, the puzzle would have two solutions (swap A and B in all four cells). Since that's impossible, the fourth cell must have a candidate other than A or B.

You can eliminate A and B from the fourth cell.

steps.title: Types of Unique Rectangles
steps.description:
There are several types:
- Type 1: One cell has extra candidates — eliminate A, B from it
- Type 2: One cell has one extra candidate C — eliminate C from cells seeing the UR
- Type 3: Extra candidates form a pair with another cell — eliminate based on the pair
- Type 4: A naked single in one cell eliminates one of the UR candidates

URs are powerful because they use meta-reasoning (the puzzle has one solution) rather than direct logic.

lessons.title: Coloring Techniques
lessons.description: Use color chains to track conjugate pairs and find contradictions.

steps.title: What is Coloring?
steps.description:
Coloring is a technique that tracks chains of conjugate pairs — pairs of cells that are the only two candidates for a number in a unit. If you color alternating cells (say, green and blue), and find that two cells of the same color share a unit, you have a contradiction — all cells of that color are wrong.

Simple Coloring: Two cells of the same color in one unit → that color is false.
Multi-Coloring: Track two separate chains and look for interactions between them.

steps.title: How to Color
steps.description:
1. Pick a number with few candidates
2. Find conjugate pairs (cells that are the only two options for that number in a row/column/box)
3. Color alternating cells: if cell A is green, its conjugate partner B is blue, B's partner C is green, etc.
4. Look for:
   - Two same-colored cells in one unit → that color is eliminated
   - A cell that sees both colors → that number can be eliminated from that cell
5. This often reveals the correct value of the colored cells

lessons.title: Almost Locked Sets (ALS)
lessons.description: ALS techniques use sets of cells that are one candidate short of being locked to find eliminations.

steps.title: What is an ALS?
steps.description:
An Almost Locked Set (ALS) is a set of N cells that collectively have N+1 candidates. For example, 2 cells with 3 candidates total, or 3 cells with 4 candidates. If you removed one candidate, the set would be 'locked' (each cell forced to a specific value).

ALS techniques look for interactions between two ALSs to find eliminations.

steps.title: ALS-XZ Rule
steps.description:
The simplest ALS technique: if you have two ALSs, and candidate X appears in both, while candidate Z appears in both, then:
- If X is true in one ALS, Z must be false in the other (and vice versa)
- Any cell that sees all instances of Z in both ALSs can have Z eliminated

This is a powerful elimination technique that generalizes many simpler patterns.

steps.title: Why ALS is Expert-Level
steps.description:
ALS techniques are hard to spot because they require identifying candidate sets that are 'almost locked.' Unlike simpler techniques that look at visible patterns, ALS requires counting candidates across groups of cells.

However, ALS can solve puzzles that no other technique can crack. Expert solvers use ALS chains and ALS-XZ to tackle the hardest puzzles in the world (like those rated 11.0+ on SudokuWiki).
